topic: Present subjunctive of ser and estar

tips.title: Que is the trigger, sea/esté is the reaction
tips.body: The subjunctive almost always shows up right after que, once the first part of the sentence has already expressed a wish, doubt, or emotion: 'I hope que you esté okay.' Spot the wish or doubt first, and the subjunctive that follows makes a lot more sense.

tips.title: WEIRDO tells you when to expect it
tips.body: A popular memory trick groups the subjunctive's triggers into WEIRDO: Wishes, Emotions, Impersonal expressions ('es importante que...'), Recommendations, Doubt/Denial, and Ojalá ('hopefully'). If your sentence fits one of those categories, expect sea or esté instead of es or está.

tips.title: Sea and esté are must-memorize irregulars
tips.body: Most subjunctive verbs are built by flipping the ending on the regular present-tense stem, but ser and estar don't play along — their subjunctive forms (sea, seas... and esté, estés...) have to be learned by heart, alongside a handful of other irregulars like dar, ir, haber, and saber.

tips.title: The ser/estar split survives into the subjunctive
tips.body: Even in this new mood, the old rule still applies: sea for identity, qualities, or things being wished for or doubted; esté for location, condition, or a temporary state someone hopes for. If you'd use ser in the present, lean toward sea here — and the same for estar and esté.

bonusCards.english: I hope you are here for the party.
It is important that we are honest.
I doubt that the store is open.
She wants the soup to be ready before we arrive.
bonusCards.answer: She wants **que** **la** soup **esté** ready before we arrive.

bonusCards.english: Study so that you are the best doctor.
